1: Released at Ramah (Ramah): a holding area for deportees north of Jerusalem. The opening temporal clause situates this after the fall but before Jeremiah's definitive movement — a second calling/release narrative. בַּחֲנִקִּים אֲסֻר בְּתוֹךְ כָּל-גָּלוּת יְרוּשָׁלַ͏ִם (bachanickim asur betokh kol-galut Yerushalayim, bound with chains among all the exiles of Jerusalem): the prophet of exile is himself counted among the exiles, chained with them — he shares the suffering of the people whose judgment he announced.
2: יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ דִּבֶּר אֶת-הָרָעָה הַזֹּאת (YHWH Elohekha dibber et-hara'ah hazot, YHWH your God spoke this disaster): a pagan commander confessing YHWH's prophetic sovereignty — 'YHWH your God pronounced this disaster.' The Babylonian acknowledges what Zedekiah's court refused to acknowledge: the disaster was YHWH's word made event. The irony is total: the foreign conqueror understands the theology; the covenant people did not.
3: כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר וַיַּעַשׂ יְהוָה (ka'asher dibber vayyaas YHWH, as he said, so YHWH has done): Nebuzaradan's creedal summary — YHWH spoke and acted exactly as he said. This is the core of prophetic theology: the word and its fulfillment are one movement. לֹא-שְׁמַעְתֶּם בְּקוֹל יְהוָה (lo-shame'tem beqol YHWH, you did not obey the voice of YHWH): the pagan officer uses the covenant-indictment formula — the shama' beqol that Deuteronomy required and Jeremiah demanded. The foreigner pronounces the verdict the people refused to hear from their own prophet.
4: הַיּוֹם פָּטַחְתִּי אֶת-הָאַזִּקִּים (hayyom patachti et-ha'aziqim, today I have released your fetters): aziqim = shackles/chains. The day of Jeremiah's release from chains is a small personal exodus — physically freed from bondage in the moment of national exile. אֶל כָּל-הַטּוֹב בְּעֵינֶיךָ לָלֶכֶת שָׁם (el kol-hattov be'einekha lalechet sham, to wherever seems good in your eyes to go): genuine freedom of choice offered by the Babylonian commander — a freedom the king of Judah never gave the prophet.
5: שׁוּב אֶל-גְּדַלְיָּהוּ (shuv el-Gedalyahu, return to Gedaliah): the use of shuv (return) here is ironic — the prophet who spent 40 years calling Israel to shuv to YHWH is now told by a Babylonian to shuv to the land. וּשְׁבֵה אִתּוֹ בְּתוֹךְ הָעָם (ushvei itto betokh ha'am, and live with him among the people): yashav (dwell/sit/remain) — Jeremiah's choice is to dwell with the remnant rather than receive Babylonian support. The prophet identifies with the people in their land even in their humiliation.
6: מִצְפָּה (Mitzpah, Mizpah): literally 'watchtower/lookout point' — the administrative capital of the post-conquest remnant community. The covenant symbol: even after the destruction of Jerusalem and the temple, YHWH has a people in the land, and Jeremiah is among them. The land promised to Abraham, which the exile has not permanently revoked, still has a remnant.
7: The captains of the forces in the open country (sarei hachayalot asher bassadeh) and their men hear the news of Gedaliah's appointment. They had avoided capture by being outside the city. The post-conquest social geography: the poorest remained (v10), the military forces hiding in the countryside return.
8: Named captains come to Gedaliah at Mizpah: Ishmael son of Nethaniah (whose name and pedigree — son of Nethaniah, Elishama — will become significant in ch41), Johanan and Jonathan sons of Kareah, Seraiah, the sons of Ephai the Netophathite, and Jezaniah son of the Maacathite. The inventory of names preserves the historical reality of the remnant community's military leadership.
9: אַל-תִּירְאוּ מֵעֲבוֹד אֶת-הַכַּשְׂדִּים (al-tire'u me'avod et-hakhashdim, do not be afraid to serve the Chaldeans): Gedaliah's foundational speech uses yare' (fear) and aved (serve) — the Deuteronomic vocabulary of covenantal service (serve YHWH; fear YHWH). He redirects this language to the political reality: the survival strategy is to serve Babylon, which is precisely what Jeremiah had been saying for decades. שְׁבוּ בָאָרֶץ (shevu va'aretz, settle/dwell in the land): the covenant promise — living in the land — is possible even under Babylonian governance if the community submits.
10: לִפְנֵי הַכַּשְׂדִּים (lifnei hakhashdim, before the Chaldeans): Gedaliah will represent the community to the Babylonian administrators. אִסְפוּ יַיִן וְקַיִץ וְשֶׁמֶן (isfu yayin veqayitz veshemen, gather wine and summer fruit and oil): the covenant land's agricultural abundance is still available — the harvest is not canceled. The return to normal agricultural life is both practical instruction and a covenant sign: the land still produces for those who remain in it.
11: Jews in Moab, Ammon, Edom, and other nations — the diaspora before the formal exile — hear that the king of Babylon has left a remnant in the land. פְּלֵיטָה (peleitah, remnant/surviving fugitives): the remnant theology — YHWH always preserves a peleitah, a surviving band (cf. Isa 37:32; Joel 2:32). Even in judgment, the covenant promise of continuity is not extinguished.
12: וַיָּשֻׁבוּ כָּל-הַיְּהוּדִים מִכָּל-הַמְּקֹמוֹת (vayashuvu kol-haYehudim mikol-hamqomot, all the Jews returned from all the places): the double kol (all) emphasizes the scope — a mini-return from exile. This small return is a foretaste of the larger return prophesied in chs 30-33. The gathering at Gedaliah's Mizpah is a type of the eschatological ingathering.
13: Johanan son of Kareah and the military captains come to Gedaliah — the scene transitions from hopeful gathering to ominous warning. The intelligence they bring (Ishmael is plotting assassination) will be refused by Gedaliah, with catastrophic consequences.
14: הֲיָדֹעַ תֵּדַע (hayado' teda', do you really know?): the interrogative with emphatic infinitive absolute — surely you know! Baalis king of Ammon (merekh Bene-Ammon) has sent Ishmael to assassinate Gedaliah. The Ammonite king's political interest: preventing a stable Judean remnant that might eventually resist Ammonite expansion into formerly Judean territory. Political instability in the region is geopolitically useful to Ammon.
15: בַּסֵּתֶר (basseter, secretly): Johanan offers to kill Ishmael privately — assassination to prevent assassination. לָמָּה יַכֶּכָּה וְנָפֹצוּ כָּל-יְהוּדָה הַנִּקְבָּצִים אֵלֶיךָ (lammah yakkekha venafotzou kol-Yehudah haniqbatzim elekha, why should he strike you down and scatter all the Jews gathered around you?): the practical argument — the remnant community is fragile; killing Ishmael would preserve it.
16: לֹא-תַעֲשֶׂה הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה (lo-ta'aseh haddavar hazzeh, you must not do this): Gedaliah's refusal is absolute. שֶׁקֶר אַתָּה דֹּבֵר אֶל-יִשְׁמָעֵאל (sheqer attah dover el-Yishma'el, you are speaking falsely about Ishmael): the tragic final word of ch40. Gedaliah's sheqer — calling Johanan's warning a lie — echoes the entire book's pattern: the truth-tellers are not believed; the covenant community repeatedly fails to hear the word that would save it. The chapter ends with Gedaliah's fatal error stated plainly, awaiting the catastrophe of ch41.
